title: Curry goat
subtitle: Slow-braised goat in Jamaican curry
excerpt: Bone-in diced goat shoulder marinated overnight in Jamaican curry powder, garlic, ginger, and scotch bonnet, then slow-braised until the meat falls from the bone and the sauce reduces to a thick, fragrant gravy. The long braise is non-negotiable: goat requires time to soften and the sauce only develops its full depth in the final 30 minutes of reduction.
sourceNotes: Curry goat is a cornerstone of Jamaican cooking with roots in the arrival of indentured South Asian workers after Emancipation in the 19th century. The Jamaican version uses a distinct curry powder blend (often Grace or Chief brand) with a higher allspice and scotch bonnet character than South Asian blends.

content.text: Goat shoulder on the bone gives the best result. The bone releases gelatin during the long braise, which thickens the sauce and gives it the characteristic body of a proper curry goat. Boneless goat or lamb shoulder can be used, but the texture of the finished sauce is different. If using lamb, the cook time reduces to about 90 minutes.

The overnight marinade is important. The curry powder and scotch bonnet need time to penetrate the meat. Do not skip the marinade and cook immediately, the flavour will be shallower. The minimum marinade time is 4 hours; overnight is better.

Combine the {{goat-shoulder}} with the {{curry-powder}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} (whole or scored), {{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Mix well, cover, and refrigerate for at least 8 hours.

Remove the goat from the marinade (reserve the marinade). Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a heavy pot over high heat and brown the goat pieces in batches until dark on all sides, about 4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes until golden. Return the goat and reserved marinade to the pot. Add the {{tomato}} and enough water to barely cover the meat (about 600 ml). Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very gentle simmer. Cover and cook for 1 hour 30 minutes. Add the {{potato}} if using and cook uncovered for a further 30 minutes, until the sauce has thickened and the meat pulls away from the bone. Remove the scotch bonnet before serving. Serve with rice and peas.

items.fix: Add more water and continue cooking. Goat can take up to 2.5 hours. A vigorous boil toughens the meat; the liquid should barely move
items.cause: Goat was not bone-in or the simmer was too vigorous

items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat to reduce. The sauce should coat a spoon thickly
items.cause: Too much water added during cooking
items.symptom: Sauce too thin at the end

items.fix: Add the scotch bonnet whole and remove it before serving for background heat without blowing heat
items.cause: Scotch bonnet broken or left in too long

content.text: Curry goat is the signature dish of Jamaican celebrations, the dish made for weddings, christenings, and New Year's Day. It is slow food in a culture that values communal cooking: a pot that takes all morning and fills the house. In Britain it appears at Caribbean restaurants and as a staple of Jamaican home cooking in the diaspora. The Jamaican curry blend is distinct from Indian or British curry powder, heavier on allspice and with more emphasis on the scotch bonnet's fruity heat.
